data.title: Hancock Whitney Corporation 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Hancock Whitney Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Amazon Q1 Preview: Macro Noise Masks Structural Efficiency Gains (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN), the stock has lost 21% in price. I consider this an exceptional buying opportunity; I am double-upgrading my rating to a Strong Buy.

Amazon entered Q1 2025 with significant momentum from a strong 2024, with $638 billion in revenue, representing 11% year-over-year growth. In addition, operating income nearly doubled to $68.6 billion. However, it now faces a complex macroeconomic backdrop. High interest rates and persistent inflation have kept consumer sentiment cautious.

With global trade also likely realigning, the isolation of China due to heavy U.S. tariffs means Amazon faces near-term headwinds until its supply chain is rebalanced. Any near-term knock to earnings guidance for Q2 or the remainder of Fiscal 2025 will almost certainly be temporary. I consider it quite likely that Big Tech companies will not provide guidance during Fiscal 2025 earnings calls and in near-term earnings reports. This also appears advisable, given the near-term rebalancing needed but also the fast structural resurgence which is likely to allow stocks to rally as soon as H2 2025 and certainly in 2026.

Q1 2025 Earnings Analysis

Amazon's Q4 2024 results provided a springboard into the new year. Revenue in Q4 hit $187.8 billion, representing 10% year-over-year growth. Operating income surged 60% year-over-year to $21.2 billion. For Q1 2025, Amazon guided for net sales between $151.0 and $155.5 billion, a 5-9% year-over-year growth forecast. This is a deceleration from the 13% a year ago, but it still represents strong underlying demand. Amazon expects Q1 operating income of $14-18 billion, approximately flat at the midpoint compared to $15.3 billion in Q1 2024.

This is a conservative outlook that likely accounts for macro uncertainty and heavy investment in new initiatives like future AI and robotics capabilities. The question in the near term is whether Amazon's relentless efficiency tailwinds can persist into 2025, offsetting any softness in consumer demand and margin pressures. Over the long term, I have no doubt that such efficiency gains will be outsized due to its position in AI fueling a robotics revolution internally.

Such AI and robotics initiatives are already being scaled into the company's logistics operations. Already, Amazon has approximately 750,000 mobile robots and tens of thousands of robotic arms in its warehouses. This will potentially generate up to $10 billion in annual savings within five years. In Q1, I'm expecting modest year-over-year growth in e-commerce revenues (mid to high single digits) as consumer demand normalizes, but outsized profit gains are likely now that the retail segments are running leaner. In Q1, I also expect North America and International trade to show healthy growth in Prime customers and units sold, even though temperance from macro factors is a real element to monitor in the short-term future.

Amazon Web Services, or AWS, is central to Amazon's growth, delivering a 19% year-over-year increase in Q4 2024 with segment revenue of $28.8 billion. In Q1 2025, AWS is likely to sustain high-teens growth, but its operating margin will face an incremental ~$0.4 billion depreciation headwind as useful-life adjustments annualize, so I'm expecting operating profit to be only slightly up year-over-year in Q1.

Amazon is quite literally all-in on AI capability. The cloud unit has launched new AI chips (Trainium2 accelerators) and foundation models (via the Amazon Nova and Bedrock services) to attract AI developers. Moreover, Amazon's $8 billion investment in Anthropic and the joint development of a supercomputer for AI training is intended to give AWS an edge in high-end AI services.

Perhaps the greatest part about Amazon's AI initiatives is that Jeff Bezos is reportedly overseeing the building of "1,000 internal AI applications" at Amazon and the scaling of AWS's AI infrastructure. As a visionary and an inventor, there is no better hand at the wheel than the company's legendary founder at this time, especially given macroeconomic concerns that require an astute leader at the helm.

Amazon's ad division is now roughly the size of YouTube's (GOOG) ad business and still growing faster than most digital ad peers. Amazon's advertising revenue has more than doubled over the past four years. Q1 advertising growth is likely to be in the mid-teen percent range year-over-year. This will likely be slightly lower than Q4 2024's rate due to a tough comparison, but still outpacing e-commerce growth. Ad monetization tends to hold up even in softer retail sales periods, with sellers and vendors competing for limited ad slots to drive sales. In addition, the company's "full-funnel" ad strategy supporting ads across its digital ecosystem gives it additional levers to pull.

Third-party seller services also grew by 9% in Q4, with room for reacceleration later in 2025 if consumer demand picks up with lower interest rates. These tailwinds can be compounded by Amazon further expanding programs like Buy with Prime, which lets non-Amazon sites use Amazon's payments and logistics. Lastly, subscription services, including Prime memberships, grew ~10% in Q4. With over 200 million Prime members globally, membership fee growth is relatively steady and provides a cushion of recurring income.

Valuation Analysis

My 12-month price target relies on Amazon's price-to-operating cash flow, as it captures the company's cash-generating strength amid heavy growth investments. In 2024, the company delivered record operating cash flow of $115.9 billion, representing 36% year-over-year growth. Currently, Amazon trades around 17x operating cash flow, which is significantly below its five-year historical average of about 26x. This is a cash-flow yield of about 5.9%, only moderately above the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y). This seems too conservative given Amazon's expanding margins that are likely to further grow with its automation initiatives scaling.

Amazon's return on invested capital has been expanding robustly. This reflects enhanced productivity from investments in AWS, logistics, and AI. Looking forward, I expect further return on invested capital gains as the company's AI initiatives mature, bolstering profitability. Additionally, I consider it likely that the macro environment stabilizes in the coming months. I also foresee it likely that the Fed will lower rates significantly in H2 2025 to spur economic recovery once geopolitical outcomes and international trade deals are more clearly established.

Even a conservative normalization to around 20x operating cash flow justifies a price target of $280 per share. With $150 billion in operating cash flow and 10.7 billion shares outstanding in 12 months' time, that places the stock price at $280 with a 20x operating cash flow multiple.

Risk Analysis

No analysis on Big Tech companies would be complete without a discussion on global trade tensions and tariff policy right now. As a retailer sourcing products worldwide, Amazon is exposed to the deterioration in U.S.-China trade relations. The Trump administration's deals with its international democratic allies right now are absolutely essential to securing Amazon's continued valuation expansion in the current geopolitical environment.

I have faith in the U.S. administration that we can work through current turmoil to a free trade bloc among democratic allies without China. However, a failure for this to be enacted could be highly constrictive for Amazon. I consider reduced demand and pressured margins possible if Trump takes too much control with a unidimensional vision for tariffs amid an attitude of U.S. protectionism. Instead, the more likely outcome which I have posited is a U.S.-led free trade bloc without China outlined in the next 90 days, gaining traction in the media and green-lighting Fed rate cuts, a stronger dollar, and the beginning of the next leg of the current AI-led bull market.

This brings us to a common risk outlined by financial analysts at the moment, which is the return on investment of AI within Big Tech companies. With Amazon in the midst of an investment super-cycle, planning over $100 billion in capital expenditures for 2025 alone, it's easy to see why the market can feel cautious on AI at times.

However, to rationally gauge the benefits of this investment, you must view it as the company building a long-term moat. AI will eventually be translated into highly effective robotics, including drone deliveries at scale across the world. Given these factors, short-term free cash flow reductions for impenetrable long-term market leadership is worth it.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

Since my last analysis of Amazon, the stock has declined by about 21%, presenting what I believe is an exceptional buying opportunity. Despite near-term macroeconomic headwinds (including persistent inflation, elevated interest rates, and U.S.-China trade tensions), Amazon's underlying fundamentals remain robust. With founder Jeff Bezos returning to provide crucial strategic oversight on more than 1,000 internal AI applications, I see Amazon rapidly evolving into an impenetrable leadership position in the digital marketplace and computing industry.

My conservative price target of $280 per AMZN share over the next 12 months is dependent on no severe near-term volatility shocks. However, even if such shocks do occur, the strategic initiatives are in place for long-term success. Logistics automation, AWS growth, and robust advertising are core factors supporting the company's long-term future. These factors are also likely to drive a structural resurgence by late 2025 and into 2026. Accordingly, I double-upgrade my rating to a Strong Buy.

data.title: Booking Holdings: Still Flying First Class (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
A Shelter For Trade Conflicts?

Last August, I extensively presented my bullish investment thesis around Booking Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:BKNG) as the leader of multiple trends coming together. These include a high prioritization of spending on experiences and traveling over material possessions among younger generations, who additionally drive the whole processing thereof towards online platforms. Booking not only stands at the intersection of both secular trends but also executes almost flawlessly, defending its position against competition.

Booking has returned +34% since then, against a zero-performance of the S&P 500 (SP500). The travel giant came back onto my radar now for two reasons.

  1. While geopolitical tensions in an even more extreme stage than today would, of course, burden the travel industry, the currently heated environment might even classify Booking Holdings as a safe haven with little tariff or trade conflict exposure nor high US dependency.
  1. Spending some time traveling the world in recent months, I noticed how I was increasingly relying on Booking as a one-stop-shop due to its user-friendliness and reliability. Meanwhile, I am enjoying the benefits of Booking’s highest loyalty tier “Genius Level 3” and understand firsthand, how this inspires further trips. Also, I was new to booking flights via Booking.com, but now it is probably my number one address for air travel as well.

Still, one should not derive investment decisions from personal experience (alone), which is why I decided to provide an update on a few key parameters and developments in this article. But at the very least, positive personal experiences partly prove the real-world quality of a stock’s services.

Why Booking Might Cope With U.S. Travel Boycotts

Over the past few weeks, multiple European countries, China and Canada have issued official travel warnings or at least advised on carefully reconsidering any journeys to the U.S. And travelers are, in fact, already voting with their feet. Overseas arrivals, Canadian or Mexican tourism to the U.S. are already down significant double-digit percentages, depending on scope (air, land) and source – but multiple sources are reporting the same trends in February and March. Looking forward, trends for Canadians booking U.S. flights over the next six months are even more deteriorating.

But there are two mitigating aspects why this might be a manageable headwind for Booking Holdings, if any.

  1. Based on Room Nights, the U.S. was Booking Holdings’ slowest growing region with mid-single digit growth in 2024, while Europe, Asia, and the Rest of the World all grew meaningfully faster. Furthermore, the U.S. contributed a low double-digit percentage to overall Room Nights. Europe leads with 50%, followed by Asia with 25%. When talking about travel boycotts to the U.S., we are therefore talking about the slowest growing and smallest individual market when measured in Booking Holdings’ Room Nights.
  2. Avoiding the U.S. as a travel destination does not mean the dollars will not be spent traveling elsewhere. This is another reason why Booking might not even be that relevant where travelers are going, as long as they keep spending.

In any case, if geopolitical tensions rise to levels we do not want to imagine, the travel industry might, of course, take some more serious hits as well.

A Reliable Travel One-Stop-Shop

Statistical Data from Booking’s latest earnings release supports my individual perception that the platform is highly successful in expanding beyond hotel booking. Sticking to my very personal experience with booking flights, we see the number of Airline Tickets sold via Booking platforms have surged year-on-year every single quarter for at least two years. Like most of Booking’s financial indicators, Airline Tickets saw accelerated growth in the most recent quarter as well.

Booking Holdings lives off Take Rates, aka the portion of the money the company keeps to itself as revenue, of the total transaction value that flows through the platforms. See below, that Take Rates show big seasonal differences with peaks every Q3 (summer) and lows every Q1. I calculated these Take Rates simplified by dividing Booking’s revenue by its Gross bookings. On a fiscal year basis, we see a recovery from 14.1% in 2022 steadily upwards to 14.3% in 2024. Booking stably defends its Take Rates, yet we do not see significant increases, indicating that the company might deal with peak pricing or bargaining power amid a competitive market.

Further notable financial information includes:

  • Booking returned almost all the company’s free cash flow to shareholders in FY2024, mainly through buybacks, of which there are more high-volume programs approved and even a small dividend. The repurchases reduced the share count by 5% to 9% YoY in recent quarters and years.
  • The company’s huge stockpile of thereby accumulated treasury shares leads to negative equity. However, due to its strong cash position, Booking Holdings carries no truly significant amount of net interest-bearing debt, which could furthermore be paid back within two years based on free cash flow alone.
  • Booking is guiding for a Gross bookings mid-point of +6% and a revenue mid-point of +3% in Q1 and mid-single digit growth rates for both metrics for the full fiscal year 2025. The bottom line is expected to grow faster than that.

Still Bullish, Temporary Hold

My long-term bullishness around Booking Holdings is hard to oversee. However, while I believe that Booking should get well through times of geopolitical turbulence if it does not surpass certain escalation levels, I would like to take this risk into account. So, despite all my bullishness, I am cautiously taking a temporary Hold stance. I often rate hold/neutral, even when my inclination is bullish or bearish because rating systems do not consider time horizons or investment strategies. My articles aim to inform, not to make decisions.

The valuation estimate of my first article landed at around $153 billion when the stock’s market cap stood at $113 billion. So, we have now reached my valuation estimate from August 2024. Using the very same model with updated figures and input parameters, I now land at $165 billion, which would currently even imply some upside beyond the normal return over time. Let us see what management has to say about the current geopolitical environment during the next earnings presentation on Tuesday, April 29.

data.title: Managing Potential Market Pivots With Options
data.description:
Introduction

As you read this, look for three takeaways:

  1. Never use puts to hedge equity positions in stressed markets when implied volatilities are high, and
  2. When market stress is evident, ratio spreads of late-dated long calls with near-dated short calls can customize a risk profile in accordance with desired objectives, and
  3. The principles underlying the assessment of option structures include an understanding of the influence of volatility's mean reversion, the probability distribution of future price outcomes, and a Black Scholes VBA function. A formula for estimating price probabilities is provided in the Appendix, along with a Black Scholes VBA function for Excel.

Discussion

Wherever you might stand on the Trump administration's policy initiatives, there's no denying markets have been in chaos. Stocks have fallen sharply since February, and bonds are falling too while behaving like speculative assets.

This is a chart of SPY; the occasional sharp reversals to the upside are typical of bear market behavior, but the trend has been sharply down since about Feb. 20.

And this chart shows last week's IEF prices, a 10-year Treasury ETF; it's not often you'll see nearly 1% price gaps in Treasury quotes.

With the past week's two inflation reports (CPI and PPI) indicating disinflation, one might expect bond prices to rally, but as I began writing this on the morning of Friday, April 11, the 10-year Treasury yield is almost 13 basis points higher. I suspect we have a lot of foreign trades in that decline. The same might be said for the declining US dollar index.

So, how would one best position in this market? I've spent most of the last 10 years studying market implications of the VIX and VX futures, as well as the underlying S&P implied volatilities that inform those symbols. The rest of this article will share some of the insights from that work.

Two Market Types

As you read this, note that the VIX reflects near-term S&P implied volatilities (IVs). Both the VIX and S&P IVs average about 20% over the long term and tend to be mean reverting. When IV's are very high, they tend to move lower, and when very low, they tend to move higher. The implications are more far-reaching than evident at first; read on.

Type-1, Hedge-Advantaged Markets

Markets can be split into two types. The most favorable type is a "hedge-advantaged" market when VIX and S&P implied volatilities are low; then a well-structured weekly hedge can often constrain loss potential while adding to mean-gain expectations when summed across the full spectrum of probabilities.

These are payout charts using XSP puts to hedge (UPRO), a 3X-leveraged ETF. They come from a Dec. 5 posting of hedge opportunities when IVs were near 10%. For context, an unhedged position of 4,000 UPRO shares was exposed to a 14% loss while these “tail risk” hedges reduced exposures to only 3% to 4%; upside was fully preserved or improved. The term “tail risk” refers to constraining left-side outcomes in these charts.

When S&P implied volatilities are about 14% or below, loss potential can typically be reduced by about 75% with well-structured weekly option strategies; at the same time, potential savings at low-side outcomes more than offset the cost of puts, so mean-gain expectations improve. The mean-reversion note that opened this section would indicate that, under Type-1 conditions, future volatility is likely to be higher than the prevailing level when hedges are placed. All of these charts assume future realized volatility will move toward the long-term mean.

As IVs grow beyond that 14% level, some sacrifice of mean-gain expectations comes along with reduced loss potential, but trade-offs are still favorable until a tipping point is reached. Then a Type-2 market is evident, and that's where we find ourselves today.

Type-2, Potential-Pivot Markets

When VIX and S&P IVs exceed 20%, markets are typically down, but per mean reversion, they're prone to an upside pivot; yet negative sentiment can prevail for indefinite periods, so prices could decline further, sometimes sharply. It's this kind of market that's most difficult to navigate, and what we have now exhibits IVs well in excess of 20%.

If you believe a bottom is forming but don't like the elevated volatility, what are your choices? Using puts as a hedge destroys gain potential since the high IVs add an abnormal expense burden, so tail-risk hedges become ineffective.

The urge to buy puts in high-stress markets is self-defeating.

This is a payout graph of a simple put structure of six-day tenor. It was run on March 27 when at-the-money implied volatilities were about 21%. Today IVs are closer to 35%, but even at 21% IVs, reducing loss exposure by about half sacrificed 72% of mean-gain expectations; the profit sacrifice spanned most of the probability spectrum.

But without loss constraints, holding a major position in risk assets is problematic given risk conditions. The VIX can go to multiples of that 20% level, and unhedged losses can become very painful.

But opportunities still exist to play in a risk-constrained manner; it just requires more complex strategies and an understanding of how volatility affects price outcomes.

This is a graph of five-day forward price probabilities derived using the formula in the Appendix and a 25% volatility assumption. With IVs at 35%, that 25% assumption relies on a large degree of mean reversion toward the sub 15% long-term observed volatility, but 35% volatilities don't usually last long. The lower realized volatility assumption will be punitive to call options' upside, and in a few paragraphs we'll look at the sensitivity to that assumption.

The gray area shows probable prices and the green/red show P&L for 400 shares of SPY referencing the right scale.

This is the payout chart for six lots of a simple $20 in-the-money call option with a long-dated Jan. '26 expiry under the same assumptions.

The long expiry and deep-in-the-money selection aim to minimize time decay. Note the higher gain potential and lower loss exposure compared to holding SPY; breakeven has risen ever so slightly from the 50% probability outcome due to the small time decay of the call.

What if volatility remained high? This comparison assumes a 37% realized volatility. The call option is still less exposed to loss than holding 400 shares of SPY, and the right-tail upside has accelerated to $31,000.

I use a VX-futures-based proprietary algorithm to measure risk, exiting at threshold levels and reentering when risks subside, but even with that advantage, I like to safely play upside reversals. It can be done effectively with option strategies that balance upside potential with limited exposure and neutral-to-favorable time decay.

Personally, I'd be uncomfortable with that one-week $15,000 to $20,000 loss exposure in this market. Also, given the current market uncertainty, I'd like to extend the win ratio from 50% to much higher. So, let's reduce it by selling some short calls along with those long-dated, deep-in-the-money positions.

These next two graphs show two of many structures where the win ratio can be modified in exchange for right- and left-tail changes.

Closing

There are many choices when faced with a stressed market. Some are poor regardless of your personal objectives, but others can constrain losses while configuring upside to your personal preference. Contrary to those who advise holding through turmoil, I prefer to either exit on threshold risk metrics or engineer a tolerable risk-return profile using option structures.

There's no need to try to get rich this particular week.

When implied volatilities are above 20%, puts are an almost universally bad idea; they destroy far more upside than their loss exposure improvement. Straight in-the-money calls can provide upside with constrained losses, but if you're concerned about market uncertainties, some combination of long-dated, in-the-money calls with near-dated at-the-money calls can provide an improved win ratio with a reasonable risk-return profile.

If you're analytical and looking for some quantitative tools, check the Appendix for how to estimate a future price distribution. There is also a very handy VBA function for Black Scholes calculations in Excel. Good analysis makes better investors.

Appendix

Estimating Price Probabilities

In Excel, for each increment of cumulative confidence from 1% to 99%, enter this formula for the potential price:

=LOGNORM.INV(Cumulative Confidence, LN(Current Price x (1 + average drift rate)), tenor volatility )

where tenor volatility = the annual volatility scaled to the horizon evaluated as follows:

Tenor Volatility = annual volatility x SQRT(Tenor/252)

Black Scholes Merton VBA

Under the Developer Tab in a macro-enabled Excel workbook, go to 'Visual Basic' and add a new module. Then in the module enter this code:

Option Explicit

Function BSMValue(S, X, r, q, tyr, sigma, i As Boolean)'returns Black-Sholes option value (call or put set by ion variable'Macro by: Brett Weese'Source: Advanced Modelling in Finance Using Excel and VBA (Jackson, Staunton)Dim ert, eqtDim DOne, DTwo, NDOne, NDTwoert = Exp(-q * tyr)eqt = Exp(-r * tyr)Select Case iCase 1 'call optionDOne = (Log(S / X) + (r - q + 0.5 * sigma ^ 2) * tyr) / _(sigma * Sqr(tyr))DTwo = (Log(S / X) + (r - q - 0.5 * sigma ^ 2) * tyr) / _(sigma * Sqr(tyr))NDOne = Application.NormSDist(DOne)NDTwo = Application.NormSDist(DTwo)BSMValue = S * ert * NDOne - X * eqt * NDTwoCase 0 'put optionDOne = -(Log(S / X) + (r - q + 0.5 * sigma ^ 2) * tyr) / _(sigma * Sqr(tyr))DTwo = -(Log(S / X) + (r - q - 0.5 * sigma ^ 2) * tyr) / _(sigma * Sqr(tyr))NDOne = Application.NormSDist(DOne)NDTwo = Application.NormSDist(DTwo)BSMValue = -S * ert * NDOne + X * eqt * NDTwoEnd SelectEnd Function

Protect and Grow Your Wealth With EZV Algorithms

Accelerate returns with dramatically less risk with EZV Algorithms, a quantitative approach derived from risk metrics embedded in the VIX futures curve. With more than 30 years of experience, Michael Gettings has developed a system that identifies high-risk intervals to avoid major drawdowns and accelerate returns on capital during rebounds. Since its 2019 inception, EZV Algorithms has more than doubled the S&P 500’s buy-and-hold returns, while reducing the worst drawdowns by 70%.

It's not a day-trading system, but a risk-assessment approach that tracks SPY, UPRO, SVXY and FBTC or IBIT, two Bitcoin ETFs. The algorithm is often supplemented with option strategies to further reduce volatility and, sometimes, to accelerate gains. By protecting against large losses and compounding gains during market recoveries, it drives stronger returns.

Start your subscription today before the price rises to $1,199 on June 2, and learn how EZV Algorithms can make you a more confident investor.

data.title: BANX: Resilient In The Face Of Volatility And Providing Steady Distributions
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Amid the ongoing equity market volatility, investors could be looking for more stable areas to invest and collect more income. No doubt, greater market volatility will inevitably lead to better opportunities to invest for some investors, but for others, focusing on steady income is more important.

That's where ArrowMark Financial Corp (NASDAQ:BANX) can come in, as a name that was quite resilient to the market volatility. Though with the tariff announcements, the fund did take a quick drop, it has been recovering since last week. Even better, they keep pumping out a steady distribution to investors. In fact, we've also seen regular special cash distributions out of this name as well, as a type of "bonus" income, thanks to strong distribution coverage driven by the income generation in the underlying portfolio.

BANX Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.71.
  • Discount/Premium: -9.4% (based on 02/29/2025 estimated NAV).
  • Distribution Yield: 9.24%.
  • Expense Ratio: 4.29%.
  • Leverage: 23.18%.
  • Managed Assets: $208 million.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

BANX's investment objective is "to provide shareholders with current income." To achieve these investment objectives, they will invest primarily in "regulatory capital securities of financial institutions."

Performance - Holding Steady

In our last update, we had a hold rating on this name due to the fund seeing its normally wide discount narrow to only a marginal level. With the latest drop, the discount had widened quite materially from where it was, making it look like a better opportunity.

Though on a total return basis, the fund has seen a decline of around 2.6%, that's been quite resilient relative to the broader equity market. That would be measured by the S&P 500 Index, which dropped nearly 10% during this same period.

That said, it is worth noting that the equity market isn't the best benchmark to compare BANX to as a fixed-income fund. It can provide some context, and the idea of today's article was looking for a resilient investment choice to withstand the greater volatility that the broader equity market is showing, which is why it is a more appropriate comparison to help illustrate that today.

As we note in all of our updates, this fund was previously focused primarily on investing in underlying bank issuances, such as preferreds and their debt. Starting in February 2020, that's when the ArrowMark team acquired the fund and started to transition into regulatory capital relief securities. We'll discuss more on those below, but that altered the fund quite a bit. They are still technically investing in "BANX," but in a different manner; it could be summed up as more investing with banks rather than in banks.

With that, we can start to use the 5-year lookback period to see how the fund is performing on a relative basis. As we can see, equities have outpaced fixed-income by a massive amount; there is no denying that.

However, we can also see that BANX has outpaced most other fixed-income investments, as measured by iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG), iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) and Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN). These are non-leveraged, passively managed indexed ETFs, making them an investable benchmark for comparison purposes. Even further, BANX has been quite competitive in terms of total share price returns compared to (SPY) and (QQQ), common measurements for the equity market performance.

We can also see that in terms of total share price returns relative to its own NAV returns, results were nearly double. That would be thanks to discount narrowing—but note that BANX only provides a monthly estimated NAV. The NAV per share for the period ended February 28, 2025, came to $22.04. That's up from the $21.80 we saw at the end of December 31, 2024. Month over month, it was a slight decline from the $22.11 saw at the end of January 31, 2025.

Given the last reported NAV, we'd arrive at a discount of roughly -9.4% currently (share price $19.97 at the time of writing), which means relative to the previous five years, the discount is not looking like a bargain but is trading around its average level. Also, considering the $0.55 paid out in distributions, we'd expect NAV could be down to $21.49 today—which would put the current estimated discount to around 7%, all else being equal.

BANX's Portfolio

BANX is a unique investment choice in the CEF space. They invest primarily in regulatory capital relief securities, and here is the description they use to help provide a broad overview of the security:

Globally, banks are encountering stricter regulations on capital requirements as regulators strive to minimize systemic risk in the banking sector. Consequently, to maintain asset levels, banks need to increase equity levels and/or use tools like regulatory capital relief securities. Regulatory capital relief transactions provide a way for banks to meet regulatory requirements while managing risk and freeing up capital for other business areas. These transactions reference a portfolio of performing loans in which investors receive a premium from the bank for assuming the credit risk.

Regulatory capital relief transactions provide a way for banks to meet regulatory requirements while managing risk and freeing up capital for other business areas. These transactions reference a portfolio of performing loans in which investors receive a premium from the bank for assuming the credit risk.

This is an area where there aren't many ways to get exposure for most retail investors that are also trading on the public market, except through BANX. It also tends to lead to what can be quite a black box given the nondisclosure agreements ("NDAs") that they have with the financial institution counterparties. It also tends to be why the fund's expense ratio is so high, as the fund is also leveraged, the fund's total expense ratio comes to 6.96%.

This can lead to a lack of transparency and some hesitation from investors considering this fund. I definitely would understand wanting to avoid an investment that isn't entirely understood, but with a rather solid track record starting to develop from this team, I think investors could start warming up. The specials could also catch some attention, but the frequency and size of the specials could slow down. We'll discuss that next.

Distribution - Steady With Specials

Understanding the BANX portfolio is important because these are also primarily floating-rate securities. With the Fed cutting 100 basis points, that is going to apply some pressure to their overall income generation. However, as we noted above, the fund has once again just recently paid out another $0.10 special because their coverage was so high in the first place between income and capital gains realized. This was on top of a $0.20 special announced and paid last December, too, just a few months ago.

To illustrate what we've seen, we can look at the last quarterly NII, which came in at $0.56 compared to the previous quarterly figure at $0.70.

At $0.56 NII, that means that the regular quarterly $0.45 distribution is still seeing coverage of nearly 125%. However, the important caveat here is that there is some variability from quarter to quarter—further, the entire 100 basis point cut the Fed has put into place also wouldn't have been reflected in this entire period yet. The fund also employs some leverage based on floating rates, which will also see their costs start to decline, helping to soften the actual overall spread narrowing we'd otherwise see.

From when rates were set at zero by the Fed through when the Fed was raising aggressively, we can clearly see the benefit this fund experienced from increased income generation.

With all that said, I suspect that overall, the distribution coverage will weaken but remain solid with enough cushion to continue supporting the current regular quarterly distribution. The specials may become less frequent, but those should always be considered a "bonus" on top of the regular.

Conclusion

At this time, BANX is once again becoming a more interesting fund to consider given the estimated widening discount. Even further, it has been relatively resilient in the face of a declining equity market that has become more volatile. When the tariffs were announced, BANX did experience a dip, but it was otherwise holding up quite well. The drop has also been recovering quite strongly since.

Overall, it can be quite appealing for income-focused investors. That is particularly true when you consider that the distribution coverage has a solid cushion, and that translates into the distribution looking like it is quite solid as well. Even with the narrowing discount, one could potentially consider dollar-cost averaging into a position.

There is one final caution, which we noted in our prior update too. That is, there was an N-2 form filed that became effective, and that means they could choose to conduct a rights offering at some point. That usually causes short-term disruptions in the share price, meaning that the price often pulls back quite meaningfully if one is announced. However, it could also be simply in place for an at-the-market offering as well, or for the potential to issue some preferred securities as well.

data.title: S&P 500: Top Of The Range, Death Cross - Sell
data.description:
Tactical Buy trade

My baseline outlook for the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY) is a recessionary bear market with the AI bubble burst and possibly the credit event related to the bursting housing bubble as the economy enters a recession. In my view, the S&P 500 earnings will decline by 10-15% in 2025 and the P/E multiple will contract, which is likely to push the S&P 500 down to 4100. Thus, my longer-term recommendation is still a Strong Sell.

However, given the recent developments with tariffs, my near-term outlook is for high volatility, which creates an opportunity for tactical trading. Thus, I issued a tactical Buy recommendation for the S&P 500 near the bottom of the range, expecting a near-term de-escalation in a trade war. This was a correct prediction, as President Trump shortly after delayed the reciprocal tariffs for 90 days.

The S&P 500 spiked by over 10% after the 90-day tariff delay announcement. However, it sold off the next day. I even recommended to Buy this "dip" as well, expecting that the Trump Put was in place, and possibly even the Fed Put, given the turbulence in the bond market. This was also a correct call, as Trump continued to roll back some tariffs, exempting some tech products from the reciprocal tariffs.

Many expected that the S&P 500 could stage another 10% on Monday in response to the tech tariff exemption announcement, primarily benefiting tech mega-caps like Apple Inc. (AAPL), which are heavily exposed to China.

However, the S&P 500 performance was muted on Monday, with a particularly muted performance in tech, as the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) finished flat. Thus, the "bounce" is likely getting exhausted, and the Tactical Buy position should be closed.

Technical picture

Technical analysis of the S&P 500 also suggests that the bounce is likely exhausted, and that the downtrend is likely to continue.

Specifically, Monday's rally sold off at Wednesday's monster rally highs, which in turn stalled at the key 5500 level resistance. The 5500 level was the key support prior to the Liberation Day selloff, now it's becoming the key resistance.

The price action established a wide range between 4800 (on futures) and 5500. We just nearly revisited the 5500 level, and failed.

In addition, it seems like the S&P500 is tracking the 20dma downtrend, which is currently also at 5500, and thus adds to the importance of the 5500 level.

Finally, the S&P500 is in the process of completing the death-cross, which is a technical term when the 50dma crosses the 200dma in a downtrend. This is also a bearish sign, and suggests that the downtrend in the SéP500 is likely to continue - and supports closing the Tactical Long position, and possibly establishing a Short position, which is consistent with my long-term Strong Sell outlook.

Bearish fundamentals

The current 20% correction can be split into two parts:

  1. The Pre-Liberation Day 10% correction since the mid-February ISM report, which indicated that "soft" data was quickly deteriorating, and pointed to a stagflationary scenario - mostly due to the expectations of tariffs policy.
  2. The Post-Liberation Day 10% selloff in response to the punitive reciprocal tariffs, supporting the worst-case scenario of stagflation, or inflationary recession.

Thus, the market still expects that the hard data will start corroborating the soft data. In other words, the market still expects a recession.

After Liberation Day, the selloff accelerated, as the probability of a recession significantly increased. However, as Trump started to de-escalate the trade war with the delays and exemptions, the market bounced. But this was a technical bounce, mostly due to short covering and some opportunistic tactical longs.

These delays and temporary exemptions are not decreasing the probability of a recession, maybe just delaying it. Thus, the market still expects a recession, which essentially caps the rally.

The fact is that there are already significant tariffs in place, as shown in the table below. Even if only the current tariffs remain, and the reciprocal tariffs are completely canceled, the economy is still very likely to enter a recession.

But it's only the tariffs. Assuming no tariffs at all, the DOGE austerity by itself could cause a mild recession, given the reversal of Biden's massive spending which boosted the economy and the labor market.

The point is that the S&P 500 earnings are still expected to grow at 8-10% in 2025, but if the recession comes as most now expect, the actual earnings growth could be -10-15% negative, and that's a sharp earnings recession likely to depress the stock market.

Valuation - still a bubble

The S&P 500 is down around 8% YTD, but it's still trading at a bubble-like valuation, with the Shiller P/E ratio above 33 as the chart below shows.

The trailing 12-month P/E ratio is at 22, while the forward P/E ratio is at 20, and that assumes almost 10% earnings growth. Both of these valuation measures point to the overvalued stock market, even after the recent selloff.

Implications

The bounce in the S&P 500 is likely exhausted, and the downtrend is likely to continue. Technically, the S&P 500 is at a key resistance, while the moving averages are forming a death cross.

Fundamentally, the S&P 500 is facing the Q1 earnings season where the component companies are likely to guide significantly lower, and the analysts are likely to significantly reduce the 2025 earnings growth projections.

However, the hard data is still not pointing to a recession, and until this happens, I expect the S&P 500 only to revisit the previous lows around 4800-5000, at which point we can re-evaluate the tactical positioning, specifically considering the Trump Put and the Fed Put.

In my view, the key data point is the weekly claims for unemployment - once this labor market measure spikes above 250K investors should brace for a recession - and they break below the 4800 lows of the S&P 500.

data.title: SBS Holdings Upgraded To Buy On Undervaluation
data.description:
In October, I covered SBS Holdings, Inc. (OTCPK:SBSOF) with a hold rating. To me, the activity in the highly competitive 3PL space in Japan and the need to grow the business through acquisitions to achieve better economies of scale and exert higher competitive pressure did not seem like very attractive elements. My price target for the stock was $25.89.

Since I published my take on SBS Holdings, the OTC ticker has lost 2.5% of its value. The more liquid listing (2384:JP) in Tokyo has gained 13.5% and 22% denominated in USD. In this report, I will discuss the most recent earnings and update my take on the company.

If you're not familiar with the activities of SBS Holdings. I recommend reading the description below sourced from my prior report.

SBS Holdings Attempts To Increase Share In A Competitive Market

SBS is a logistics company specializing in air and ocean freight forwarding to and from Japan with growing activity to create a network in Asia through regular logistics support and 3PL (third-party logistics services).

The company also provides import and export clearance services, package design, logistics technology, and warehouse management including utilization of robotics and AI.

The logistics chain and primarily the 3PL market is highly fragmented, leading to many active players with a small market share in a very competitive market. SBS Holdings' market share also shows this. The company has a market share of slightly more than 5% to 6% and has been working for years on expanding its market share from around 2% through M&A. 3PL is one of the company's focus businesses - the other two businesses are international logistics and e-commerce logistics.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For SBS Holdings?

The obvious risk currently is the trade turmoil that significantly affects consumer confidence and may result in the slowing of economic growth. This subsequently can affect unit freight rates and volumes for logistics providers. It's not easy to take cost out of the system, which means that an abrupt sentiment change or economic downturn can adversely impact margins.

The company is aiming to grow through M&A, which would allow it to achieve better economies of scale. However, while macroeconomic pressure does provide opportunities to acquire companies cheaper, M&A is not going to offset significant macroeconomic pressure.

Earnings Analysis Shows Domestic Sales Growth But Volume-Driven Profit Impact

The revenue bridge above looks a bit messy, but provides a very good overview of the moving bits and pieces. Total revenues increased 3.8% to ¥448.1 billion yen. Property management sales increased 29% and accounted for 25% of the increase primarily realized through selling properties.

Logistics revenues increased 2.9% and this growth was achieved in the second half of the year. Overall, we see that the domestic market showed significant increases in revenues through customer expansion despite lower freight volumes. Overseas revenues decreased in H1 due to lower freight rates and volumes and in H2 the freight volumes recovered somewhat.

While revenues have gone up, the profitability tanked 10.2% to ¥17.7 billion yen. This indicates a margin decrease from 4.6% to 3.9%. The cost of establishing new offices played a major role in the margin erosion. If we were to exclude this non-recurring item, the margins would be 4.2% with the lower margin driven by lower freight volumes.

So, overall the business seems to be expanding, and the company has been able to rationalize prices and expand the customer base, but freight volumes pressured the profits. This shows how freight volume decreases can make it a challenge for a business to stabilize the already thin margins.

SBS Holdings Lowers Expectations For 2025

For 2025, SBS Holdings has decreased its expectations slightly from its original plan. That's not so much driven by the current trade turmoil but by low ocean freight rates after those rates had been elevated due to the situation in the Red Sea. Furthermore, revenues from semiconductor logistics have been revised downward while the opening of new offices also pressures the cost profile.

SBS Holdings Upgraded To Buy

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I'm not quite charmed by the idea of SBS Holdings having to grow through acquisitions to grow its margins. However, at the same time the company is expected to see EBITDA growth of 6% annually and free cash flow is expected to grow around 19.1% annually. Combined with the company’s current undervaluation it leads to a price target of $29.64 without even factoring in growth in the years ahead. We also have not used the premium that SBS Holdings trades at compared to the peer group.

Keeping all of this in mind I'm upgrading the stock to buy.

Conclusion: SBS Holdings Upside In A Challenging Market

I previously had a hold rating on the stock and perhaps the results posted for 2024 also provide some support to that. Revenues increased, but earnings went down on lower volume and expansion costs.

I'm generally not a big fan of companies having to acquire businesses to grow a low-margin business out to something decent. While that's not the kind of investment opportunity that I'm looking for, the reality is that SBS Holdings stock is undervalued against its median trading multiple and is also trading at a discount compared to peers. The trade risk we're currently seeing may put pressure on the stock price, but I do believe that from a valuation perspective, a buy rating is actually appropriate and perhaps that should have been the rating in my prior report on SBS Holdings as well.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Southeast Asia Tariffs Won't Last: Deckers Outdoor Stock Is A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Learning how to cut through the noise and look toward the medium-term future is an important skill for investors. One of the few areas in which individual investors can have an advantage over Wall Street traders is that they can zoom out and examine the longer-term, bigger picture of human and social behavior rather than being confined by short-term math assumptions. Sometimes we forget that business isn't all about numbers. At the end of the day, there are trillions of people buying and selling goods and services based on how they feel, and it's these social transactions that produce the numbers. At its base, when people are forced to pay more for something than they need to pay or are used to paying, they leave that transaction unhappy and frustrated because it diminishes the standard of living they were accustomed to.

In this article, I will share my deep cyclical investing strategy for Deckers Outdoor Corporation (NYSE:DECK) stock. It will be a combination of fundamental analysis and long-term stock price analysis, but it will also share my understanding of the types of things that can really anger people, and why those things likely mean the huge tariffs we've seen announced for Southeast Asia, likely won't stay for long. (Interestingly, I wrote this intro before the recent 90-day reciprocal tariff pause, the reversal of tariffs on smartphones, computers, chips, etc. from China, and the announcement that the reversal will only be temporary).

Decker's Outdoors makes popular shoe brands like Uggs, Hoka, and Teva. Footwear is likely to be one of my bigger industry allocations during the current downturn because they have a combination of cyclicality and necessity. The cyclicality and economic sensitivity over the short term can cause downturns in earnings and stock price that ultimately turn out to be temporary, which creates good opportunities for longer-term investors who are willing to wait for a recovery.

Examining Historical Earnings Cyclicality

I start virtually every stock analysis by examining the historical earnings patterns of the business. This provides me with information about how steady, cyclical, erratic, or, sometimes, nonexistent earnings have been. I've found that about 80% of the time, a company's earnings history is useful for estimating the future of the stock, and about 20% of the time, things really are different (beyond the usual things I check for), and there is a break from past patterns.

In Decker's case, I will use "Basic Earnings" history because it shows the earnings downturns more clearly, and the stock price has tended to follow the basic earnings more than the adjusted earnings.

In the FAST Graph above, the dark green shaded area represents Deckers' historical earnings per share. Usually, what happens with companies like this is when it appears growth is going too slow, they go out and try to add new brands to spur new growth, so they make acquisitions. That is likely what explains the decline in basic earnings in 2013 when they bought Hoka (adjusted earnings didn't fall). Eventually, the Hoka brand paid off, but it, along with some other acquisitions during that time, took about 8 years to show up in basic earnings enough to produce new earnings highs. During this time, the stock price tended to follow these basic earnings trends. So, these acquisition cycles are one way to look at things for a smaller footwear business. (However, when the business gets larger, they might not move the needle as much).

Perhaps what is more interesting is that during the great recession, earnings didn't fall at all for Deckers. Granted, it was a smaller company than with fewer brands, so sometimes niche products like Tevas can do well even when the macro environment is poor. Now that Deckers is bigger, I expect earnings will likely be more sensitive to the macroeconomic environment than in 2008/9, but there hasn't been much historical evidence for that, yet, even if we do have evidence of product and acquisition cycles affecting earnings. What we do have clear evidence for is that over the long term, earnings have risen and recovered from any setbacks the business might have experienced. This lets me know that this is a quality business that has a high probability of recovering from any downturns it experiences. They have good brands that people want to buy (as long as people have the money to do it).

Historical Price Cycles

Using some basic logic described in the previous segment, when the historical earnings cyclicality isn't very clear (but the business still looks good), as with Deckers, I revert to historical price cycles to see how the market reacted to the macro cycles in the past. Stock price drawdown charts are a good way to do that once we know we are dealing with a quality business.

Above is a drawdown chart for Deckers from 2004 through today. We have two general types of drawdowns. From 2004-2014, the stock price fell more than -60%. These drawdowns included the Great Recession, and the 2013-2014 period I pointed out earlier. Usually, we would expect the stock to fall -65% to -75% during one of these declines, regardless of what earnings are doing. So, we know from examining the positive long-term earnings trend that Deckers is a quality business. Fundamental analysis tells that. But in the short term, the stock price doesn't follow earnings if there are macro threats or acquisition cycles. That means things like P/E ratios will not be as good of a guide for when to buy the stock as price drawdowns will.

In the chart above, I simply changed the time frame of the drawdown chart to 2015-2025. During these non-recessionary declines, the stock price tends to fall between -45% and -55% off its highs before recovering. When you buy a stock -50% off its high (imagine a $100 stock falling to $50, and then recovering to $100), you make a 100% return. If we took the low end of this range and bought after a -45% decline, that produces about an 80% return if the stock price recovers.

An investor who used this simple approach with Deckers over the past 10 or 11 years could have done this 3 times. So, they could have turned $100 into $180 from 2015-2018 and taken profits. Then they could have taken $180 in 2020 and turned it into $324 in less than a year. And finally, in 2022, an investor could have taken $324 and turned it into $583 in a little over a year. This is nearly a 500% return in 10 years. And that doesn't include the money an investor could have made when not invested in the stock.

This raises the question of why I have never owned Deckers until now. There are two reasons for that. The first is that usually when I'm scanning the market for quality deep cyclical stocks (and I've scanned thousands, one by one, over the years), I am first looking for deep adjusted earnings cyclicality, and Decker's adjusted earnings haven't been deeply cyclical.

Historically, the deepest decline in adjusted earnings was -32% in 2012, which I would consider moderately cyclical (they decided to make acquisitions after that year, likely to spur growth). So, when I first looked at Deckers' stock, I didn't treat it like a deeply cyclical business. It was only later when I realized that, whether it truly was deeply cyclical or not, the market treated it that way. For example, in 2022, the stock price fell about -45% even as earnings grew +19%. When combined with a similar stock price reaction in 2008/9, I could tell earnings were not a good way to determine a good buy price for the stock.

The second reason I didn't buy it was that, usually, I don't buy deep cyclical stocks unless they are at least -50% off their highs, and in the past two down cycles, in 2020 and 2022, it didn't fall that far. Now, however, as we saw from the drawdown chart above, the stock price is more than -50% off its high, which means it's reasonable to expect a 100% return from the stock if macro conditions return to something similar to the past. It's just a matter of how long it might take for that to happen.

My investing approach for deep cyclicals attempts to find historical patterns and then assumes they will roughly repeat. However, I do check several things in order to see if there are obvious signs that this cycle really might be different and not repeat. I call these checks "impairment tests" and usually, they take the form of a question. In the next section, I will run through this list of tests with Deckers Outdoor.

Impairment Tests

Are revenues this cyclical peak higher than the last one?

While revenues stalled from 2015-2019 or so, they actually haven't been cyclical (likely due to acquisitions, which boosted revenues during the weak period). So far, the market is predicting a decline that hasn't happened yet. This metric looks fine.

Could the business have a hidden fatal flaw?

Deckers have been publicly traded since 1993, and so far, a fatal flaw has yet to be exposed during previous downturns. The elephant in the room right now is obviously tariffs. The market seems to think that because of DECK's reliance on labor from Southeast Asia, like Vietnam, it might be very exposed to Trump's new tariffs. Almost all of Decker's decline has come after inauguration day.

I am willing to bet we will not be keeping the current tariff levels on Vietnam for long. First, we are not going to manufacture shoes in quantity in the U.S. anytime soon. It's a joke to think this will happen. Nobody wants to work in shoe factories, and robots aren't going to magically appear out of thin air. Meanwhile, most of Trump's voter base is relatively poor. Once moms of families already strapped for cash go back-to-school shopping in a few months, I expect there will be a true revolt the likes of which we have never seen before in this country. The truth is that we need affordable shoes in the U.S. more than Vietnam needs us. So, this tariff situation will be worked out. Probably sooner rather than later.

Is there a clear and disruptive threat to its core business?

No. They have been taking market share from competitors like NIKE, Inc. (NKE) for years now.

Has the stock experienced a recent super-cycle?

This is more difficult to tell. The COVID-stimulus boom certainly helped businesses like this, and that is worth paying attention to. If the stock was more overvalued than it was before previous drawdowns, it might draw down further than in the past. I have a couple of ways to examine this question. The first is a log-scale price chart.

While the stock has certainly had a good run and was solidly at the top of a cycle, it doesn't look much worse than the 2008 and 2012 peaks to me.

The second thing I check for is peak P/E ratios before the downcycle starts.

The peak P/E using weekly FAST Graph data was about 30 before the 2008 and 2022 declines, while higher at 38 for the current decline. This might mean the stock could fall farther than in the past, especially if there is a recession. When we combine this with the deeper historical drawdowns that occurred before 2015, I think investors should be prepared that if the macroeconomy does have a downcycle, the stock could fall about -50% deeper, to -75% off its high.

Is management corrupt or incompetent?

I think management has been great as far as I can tell.

How is the company's debt-to-equity ratio compared to previous cycles?

Debt doesn't look like a concern at all.

Has the price dropped enough to produce alpha in the past?

I'm going to test two different levels with this back test. The first is the -50% decline level, and the next, is where I will add to my position, at the -70% decline level. We only have the 2008 recession to use as a guide since DECK hasn't had a cycle where it has fallen more than -45% since then.

Deckers would have become a buy on 10/7/08, and if held a little over 2 years, would have fully recovered the old highs, producing a 41% annual return, which was about 4x better than the S&P 500. So, even though the stock would have fallen an additional -50% after the initial purchase, it would have still produced very good medium-term returns.

If an investor had made a second purchase after a -70% decline, they would have bought on 11/20/08 and held for just short of 2 years before the recovery, which would have produced a 237% return, again about 4x better than the S&P 500. So, really, any purchase of Deckers' stocks once it fell more than -50% off the highs would have handily beaten the index and also produced outstanding absolute returns for investors.

Deckers passes this test.

Conclusion And Buying Strategy

While the macro environment for the wider economy is very bad right now, and while Deckers did start its decline from a fairly expensive peak, building up to a 2% portfolio-weighted position while the stock is between -50% and -70% off its highs is a reasonable strategy for investors aiming for above average medium-term returns over the next 5 years. Much of the current price decline is the result of tariff threats that have almost no chance of sticking in any significant way. However, I do think the destabilizing effect of President Trump's rhetoric and the subsequent rise in long-duration US bond yields has a very high probability of causing a recession. My strategy is to start buying at today's levels, and to layer down should the price drop further, not exceeding a 2% initial portfolio weighting.

data.title: Ray Dalio Warns Of 'Something Worse Than A Recession' - Our Approach
data.description:
While many economists and investors are concerned about the impact that tariffs may have on economic growth in the near term, potentially driving the economy into a recession, billionaire investor and legendary hedge fund manager Ray Dalio is concerned about something much worse than a recession potentially coming out of this ongoing trade conflict, stating recently:

Right now, we are at a decision-making point and very close to a recession, and I’m worried about something worse than a recession if this isn’t handled well.

In this article, I will detail what that is and how we are investing as a result.

Mr. Dalio's Biggest Concern

Ray Dalio is worried about the very value of the dollar itself collapsing, as he sees a number of crises potentially coming to a head simultaneously as a result of the U.S.'s tariffs on China, which could lead to a major collapse of the global economic order. In particular, he sees the runaway debt of both the U.S. and China, along with a breakdown of the global multilateral trade order, leading to a de-dollarization of the global economy, which in turn would lead to soaring interest rates for the U.S., even as it has a spiraling deficit and interest expense that is already larger than its national defense budget. He sees a risk that Trump's tariffs may prompt China, along with enough other countries, to reject U.S. dollars as a reserve currency, effectively finding an alternative, or a set of alternatives, to the existing order.

Not only would such a development send shockwaves throughout the global economy by causing the value of the dollar to plummet, but it would cause interest rates to soar in the United States as Treasury bonds would come flooding back due to countries like Japan, China, and others selling their holdings and global demand for the U.S. dollar drying up. This in turn would send interest rates through the roof, which would not only wreak further havoc on the U.S. budget, but it would also harm financial markets in the United States by driving up interest rates, which in turn would lead to severe pain on several industries that are heavily reliant on debt, such as real estate, with otherwise blue-chip REITs like Realty Income (O) likely suffering significant pain due to a soaring cost of capital and plummeting real estate values. Housing would also suffer because mortgage rates will skyrocket, making the already tough housing market even tougher.

Meanwhile, that might force the Fed's hand to start buying bonds aggressively once again to keep interest rates somewhat under control, but that in turn would likely cause inflation to re-spike, especially since a lot of dollars would already be coming back to the U.S. from overseas holders. This could cause a hyperinflationary scenario where the Fed is unable to do anything about long-term interest rates because of the market's loss of confidence in the value of the dollar. Additionally, we would see not only runaway inflation, but potentially a major economic downturn as well, due to disrupted global trade as well as chaotic capital markets caused by the soaring inflation and interest rates.

While this is obviously a doomsday scenario, Mr. Dalio is not wrong in saying that recent developments have made it increasingly possible. Sure, a strong case can be made that President Trump's efforts are badly needed to create a more balanced and sustainable economy in the United States and the broader world. In particular, you can argue the efforts to bring back the manufacturing capability in strategic industries like ships, chips, automobiles, pharmaceuticals, and select other industries are essential for ensuring long-term national sovereignty and security for the United States.

However, at the same time, it is also impossible to argue, as Mr. Dalio points out, that this is a very risky gambit and that—while if it is successful, the long-term rewards will be very worth whatever near-term pain it causes—there is a very real possibility that it will not succeed. If it fails, it could actually end up accelerating the decline and collapse of the global order and the U.S. economy.

Investor Takeaway

So what does this mean for dividend investors like us? Well, we are not macro investors, but at the same time we cannot be completely ignorant of macro factors either. As a result, we are not running for the hills and hiding in our bunker because, as we pointed out earlier, this is simply just one possible macro outcome of many. However, the growing possibility of it and the turmoil that we saw in bond markets already just in response to the initial round of this trade war taking shape show that we should not be naive and assume that everything will remain fine forever.

Therefore, while we continue to focus on generating attractive and sustainable income alongside maximizing long-term total return and risk-adjusted performance in our portfolios, we are also putting increasing focus on the risk side of the equation. In particular, this means that we are increasing our focus on businesses that have very strong balance sheets and durable and defensive business models that have some inflation protections built in. In particular, we like midstream businesses like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) due to their stellar balance sheets and inherent inflation protection and recession resistance.

We also like infrastructure companies like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP, BIPC) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP, BEPC) for similar reasons because they have defensive business models that also have significant inflation indexation of their cash flows as well as very strong BBB+ credit ratings and low levels of recourse debt, which should set them up to survive better than most in the event of prolonged bond market turmoil and/or economic downturn. In addition, both BIP and BEP are well diversified across the globe, which reduces their U.S. dollar concentration risk.

Last but not least, we are also increasingly focused on businesses that have real assets and will produce physical materials because these should hold up best in an environment where the dollar collapses in value because these assets are critical resources and will be in demand regardless of what the global economic and political system is made up of. This includes energy production companies (XLE), critical residential real estate (VNQ), and an increasing emphasis on precious metals like gold (GLD) and silver (SLV) bullion leases as well as undervalued gold mining stocks (GDX) that have little to no debt and significant free cash flow profiles for the foreseeable future. In an increasingly uncertain world, these are the sorts of opportunities we are loading up on at High Yield Investor.

data.title: The Trade Desk: As Cheap As Ever, Time To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Trade Desk, Inc. (NASDAQ:TTD) has been hit hard this year. After failing to hit guidance for the first time in many years, the company was met by the ongoing tariff dispute. The net result is that what was once an ultra-premium valued stock has now curiously found itself trading at more than reasonable valuations. I am not as confident as management that growth can re-accelerate meaningfully, but the strong balance sheet and cheap valuation more than offset such fears. Make no mistake, this is a solid buying opportunity for an important enabler of the digital advertising revolution. I am upgrading TTD to a buy rating.

TTD Stock Price

I last covered TTD in September 2023, when I downgraded the stock as I made the case that the stock was “priced for perfection.” At one point, the stock had risen over 50%, but as of recent prices, the stock is down 40% and has underperformed the broader market by around 60%. The stock has now been “dead money” over nearly the last 5 years.

The fundamentals always matter at some point, which in part explains the brutal drop, but also might foretell a future recovery.

TTD Stock Key Metrics

TTD is the largest independent demand-side platform (DSP), meaning that it helps advertisers to place ads across a variety of platforms. I like to think of TTD as being similar to “The Shopify of Advertising.”

TTD has historically viewed the growth of connected TV as being one of their more promising growth opportunities, but recently Amazon (AMZN) has emerged as a key competitor in this space. It is possible that competitive fears have contributed to the re-rating downward.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 22% YoY revenue growth to $741 million, falling short of guidance for $756 million. This was the company’s first time in 8 years to miss guidance.

The company also missed guidance on the profitability front, generating $350 million in adjusted EBITDA versus the $363 million guidance.

The company ended the quarter with $1.9 billion of cash versus no debt.

Management guided for the first quarter to see at least $575 million in revenue (representing 17% growth) and $145 million in adjusted EBITDA. It is possible that the market did not like the company’s dip below a 20% growth rate profile.

On the conference call, management was adamant that their guidance miss was not due to competition of an overestimated market but rather due to “a series of small execution missteps.” Management showed confidence that they can accelerate top-line growth over time, noting that they still have only tapped 1% of the “$1 trillion TAM.”

Is TTD Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

After this year’s volatility, the stock traded hands at around 27x earnings. That represents a steep discount to the 84x 5-year average earnings multiple, but I caution that the stock had traded at ultrarich valuations heading into this year.

The stock traded hands at around 8x sales, with consensus estimates expecting no deceleration in top-line growth over the next decade. Consensus estimates look too optimistic, but I again note that the stock trades at a huge discount to the 24x 5-year average sales multiple.

I find it unlikely for TTD to return to its former valuation multiples, as I suspect that at least some of that valuation was earned from the company having been an indisputable “beat and raise” warrior, a title which it has since lost. The ongoing market turmoil might lead to declining growth rates in the near term. Even so, I find the stock quite attractive here at just over 8x sales. The company generated a 41% adjusted EBITDA margin in the last full year. I expect the company to sustain at least a 35% net margin over the long term. That places the current valuation at around 23x long-term earnings.

It bears repeating that TTD is still a rather small company, even after growing its revenues by over 10x over the last handful of years. I see the company continuing to grow rapidly as it helps consolidate the market outside of the tech titans. Even if we factor a deceleration in top-line growth into the 15% profile, a 1.5x to 2x price to earnings growth ratio (PEG ratio) would imply a valuation of between 22x and 30x earnings, or a stock price of $47 to $61 per share. The stock looks priced to deliver solid annual returns in the 15% range, even before accounting for any multiple expansions.

TTD Stock Risks

It is possible that TTD is being disrupted by competition. While the company has historically competed well against the likes of Alphabet (GOOGL), that does not imply that the company will be able to continue to do so in the future, let alone with the new AMZN threat. The stock might continue to be volatile in a market downturn, though the company’s strong balance sheet and GAAP profitability may offer some downside support. The stock is still not yet “dirt cheap” and it is unclear where top-line growth rates will bottom.

TTD Stock Conclusion

TTD has seen a dramatic fall from grace. The guidance miss and tariff turmoil have increased market uncertainty and risk premiums. The stock has fallen too much though and is looking attractively valued even if management is unable to re-accelerate top-line growth. The company’s strong balance sheet, GAAP profitability, and low valuation are all traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.
